title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 09

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Bagan, Myanmar, c. 1220: “The king grants rice fields and palm groves to support the monastery. Scribes record the boundaries, monks chant blessings for the realm, and village headmen must keep the irrigation channels clear.” What was the most likely purpose of the inscription?

choices.A: To promote the end of Buddhist monastic influence
choices.B: To present the ruler as a patron of religion, agriculture, and local order
choices.C: To announce the replacement of agrarian taxation by factory wages
choices.D: To argue that Bagan had no written administration

questions.correctAnswerText: To present the ruler as a patron of religion, agriculture, and local order
questions.explanation: The inscription links royal authority to monasteries, land grants, scribes, monks, village headmen, and irrigation.

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Bagan, Myanmar, c. 1220: “The king grants rice fields and palm groves to support the monastery. Scribes record the boundaries, monks chant blessings for the realm, and village headmen must keep the irrigation channels clear.” Which phrase best supports the claim that Buddhist institutions helped legitimize rule?

choices.A: “Monks chant blessings for the realm”
choices.B: “Village headmen”
choices.C: “Irrigation channels”
choices.D: “Record the boundaries”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Monks chant blessings for the realm”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly connects Buddhist religious authority to the legitimacy of the realm.

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Bagan, Myanmar, c. 1220: “The king grants rice fields and palm groves to support the monastery. Scribes record the boundaries, monks chant blessings for the realm, and village headmen must keep the irrigation channels clear.” Which broader context best explains the reference to irrigation channels?

choices.A: Mainland Southeast Asian states often depended on water management for rice agriculture.
choices.B: Bagan’s rulers abandoned farming after 1200.
choices.C: Irrigation was used only in Atlantic plantation colonies.
choices.D: Southeast Asia lacked organized village agriculture.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mainland Southeast Asian states often depended on water management for rice agriculture.
questions.explanation: Irrigation channels and rice fields reflect environmental management for agriculture.

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Bagan, Myanmar, c. 1220: “The king grants rice fields and palm groves to support the monastery. Scribes record the boundaries, monks chant blessings for the realm, and village headmen must keep the irrigation channels clear.” The inscription most directly suggests which social arrangement?

choices.A: A society without religious specialists or local leaders
choices.B: A political order linking ruler, monks, scribes, village headmen, and cultivators
choices.C: A merchant republic independent of religious authority
choices.D: A nomadic confederation without settled agriculture

questions.correctAnswerText: A political order linking ruler, monks, scribes, village headmen, and cultivators
questions.explanation: The inscription shows a layered society organized around religious, administrative, and agricultural roles.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Norse settlements in Greenland, c. 1200–1400: “Stone farm buildings, church ruins, cattle byres, and imported European objects appear alongside evidence of shortened growing seasons, dependence on hay, and limited adaptation to Arctic marine resources.” Which interpretation is best supported by the description?

choices.A: Norse Greenlanders adapted perfectly to Arctic conditions without difficulty.
choices.B: Norse settlements combined European cultural practices with environmental vulnerability in the North Atlantic.
choices.C: Greenland settlements were founded by industrial fishing companies.
choices.D: Norse Greenland had no connection to Europe after settlement.

questions.correctAnswerText: Norse settlements combined European cultural practices with environmental vulnerability in the North Atlantic.
questions.explanation: Churches, byres, and imported goods show European practices, while short growing seasons and limited adaptation show vulnerability.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Norse settlements in Greenland, c. 1200–1400: “Stone farm buildings, church ruins, cattle byres, and imported European objects appear alongside evidence of shortened growing seasons, dependence on hay, and limited adaptation to Arctic marine resources.” Which evidence best supports the claim that Greenland settlements remained connected to wider exchange networks?

choices.A: “Imported European objects”
choices.B: “Cattle byres”
choices.C: “Shortened growing seasons”
choices.D: “Arctic marine resources”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Imported European objects”
questions.explanation: Imported objects provide direct evidence of continued exchange beyond Greenland.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Norse settlements in Greenland, c. 1200–1400: “Stone farm buildings, church ruins, cattle byres, and imported European objects appear alongside evidence of shortened growing seasons, dependence on hay, and limited adaptation to Arctic marine resources.” The settlement pattern most directly illustrates which broader relationship?

choices.A: Long-distance settlement could be limited by environmental conditions and subsistence choices.
choices.B: Trade networks eliminated all environmental constraints.
choices.C: Arctic regions were easier for agrarian settlement than temperate regions.
choices.D: Imported goods always guaranteed political stability.

questions.correctAnswerText: Long-distance settlement could be limited by environmental conditions and subsistence choices.
questions.explanation: Short seasons, hay dependence, and limited adaptation show how environment shaped settlement survival.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Norse settlements in Greenland, c. 1200–1400: “Stone farm buildings, church ruins, cattle byres, and imported European objects appear alongside evidence of shortened growing seasons, dependence on hay, and limited adaptation to Arctic marine resources.” Which broader cultural context best explains the presence of church ruins?

choices.A: Norse settlers brought Christian institutions and European cultural practices into North Atlantic communities.
choices.B: Buddhism became the main religion of Greenland before 1450.
choices.C: Greenland settlements rejected all connections to European identity.
choices.D: Christian institutions appeared in Greenland only after 1900.

questions.correctAnswerText: Norse settlers brought Christian institutions and European cultural practices into North Atlantic communities.
questions.explanation: Church ruins show the transfer of Christian institutions to North Atlantic settlements.

questions.prompt: Originalized travel account from the Maldives, c. 1350: “The islanders follow Muslim judges, yet women move freely in the markets and ships arrive with rice from India, cloth from the west, and cowrie shells gathered for export. The ruler honors the qadi when merchants dispute contracts.” Which audience would most likely have found this account useful?

choices.A: Merchants, scholars, and officials interested in Indian Ocean island societies
choices.B: Factory inspectors regulating industrial labor
choices.C: Spanish officials governing Andean mining districts
choices.D: Cold War generals planning proxy conflicts

questions.correctAnswerText: Merchants, scholars, and officials interested in Indian Ocean island societies
questions.explanation: The account describes trade, legal practices, contracts, and island social life.

questions.prompt: Originalized travel account from the Maldives, c. 1350: “The islanders follow Muslim judges, yet women move freely in the markets and ships arrive with rice from India, cloth from the west, and cowrie shells gathered for export. The ruler honors the qadi when merchants dispute contracts.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the Maldives participated in regional exchange?

choices.A: “Women move freely”
choices.B: “Muslim judges”
choices.C: “Cowrie shells gathered for export”
choices.D: “The ruler honors the qadi”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Cowrie shells gathered for export”
questions.explanation: Cowrie shells gathered for export directly show participation in regional trade.

questions.prompt: Originalized Durrani Afghan royal order, c. 1760: “The chiefs shall bring horsemen when summoned, and the tax farmers shall send revenue from Kandahar and Kabul to the royal camp. Tribal honor is respected, but no road toll may be taken without the shah’s seal.” What was the most likely purpose of the order?

choices.A: To abolish tribal authority throughout Afghanistan
choices.B: To coordinate tribal military service, taxation, and royal authority
choices.C: To replace cavalry with industrial rail transport
choices.D: To end all trade through Afghan routes

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate tribal military service, taxation, and royal authority
questions.explanation: The order links chiefs, horsemen, tax farmers, revenue, roads, tolls, and the shah’s seal.

questions.prompt: Originalized Durrani Afghan royal order, c. 1760: “The chiefs shall bring horsemen when summoned, and the tax farmers shall send revenue from Kandahar and Kabul to the royal camp. Tribal honor is respected, but no road toll may be taken without the shah’s seal.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the ruler tried to limit local autonomy?

choices.A: “Chiefs shall bring horsemen”
choices.B: “Tribal honor is respected”
choices.C: “No road toll may be taken without the shah’s seal”
choices.D: “Kandahar and Kabul”

questions.correctAnswerText: “No road toll may be taken without the shah’s seal”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows the shah limiting local control over revenue collection.

questions.prompt: Originalized Durrani Afghan royal order, c. 1760: “The chiefs shall bring horsemen when summoned, and the tax farmers shall send revenue from Kandahar and Kabul to the royal camp. Tribal honor is respected, but no road toll may be taken without the shah’s seal.” Which broader context best explains the importance of road tolls?

choices.A: Afghan rulers sought to control revenue from routes linking Central, South, and Southwest Asia.
choices.B: Afghanistan had no overland trade routes before 1900.
choices.C: Road tolls were a feature only of Atlantic maritime empires.
choices.D: Industrial factories replaced caravan routes by 1500.

questions.correctAnswerText: Afghan rulers sought to control revenue from routes linking Central, South, and Southwest Asia.
questions.explanation: Road tolls mattered because Afghan territories sat along important overland routes.

questions.prompt: Originalized Durrani Afghan royal order, c. 1760: “The chiefs shall bring horsemen when summoned, and the tax farmers shall send revenue from Kandahar and Kabul to the royal camp. Tribal honor is respected, but no road toll may be taken without the shah’s seal.” Which statement best evaluates the political system described?

choices.A: It depended on balancing tribal elites with attempts at royal centralization.
choices.B: It eliminated military obligations for regional chiefs.
choices.C: It rejected all taxation as illegitimate.
choices.D: It was a democratic republic based on universal suffrage.

questions.correctAnswerText: It depended on balancing tribal elites with attempts at royal centralization.
questions.explanation: The order respects tribal honor but asserts royal control over tolls, military service, and revenue.

questions.prompt:
Table: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, simplified political pattern
Institution or group | Role
Elected king | Required noble consent for major decisions
Sejm | Noble assembly with legislative influence
Magnate families | Controlled large estates and private forces
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: The Commonwealth combined monarchy with powerful noble institutions.
choices.B: The king ruled without elite constraints.
choices.C: Peasants controlled the Sejm through universal suffrage.
choices.D: Magnate families were excluded from politics.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Commonwealth combined monarchy with powerful noble institutions.
questions.explanation: The elected king, Sejm, noble consent, and magnate power show monarchy constrained by aristocratic institutions.

questions.prompt:
Table: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, simplified political pattern
Institution or group | Role
Elected king | Required noble consent for major decisions
Sejm | Noble assembly with legislative influence
Magnate families | Controlled large estates and private forces
Which evidence best supports the claim that aristocratic elites limited royal power?

choices.A: “Elected king”
choices.B: “Required noble consent for major decisions”
choices.C: “Large estates”
choices.D: “Private forces”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Required noble consent for major decisions”
questions.explanation: Noble consent directly shows elite limits on royal power.

questions.prompt:
Table: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, simplified political pattern
Institution or group | Role
Elected king | Required noble consent for major decisions
Sejm | Noble assembly with legislative influence
Magnate families | Controlled large estates and private forces
Which economic pattern most directly strengthened magnate power in this period?

choices.A: Large grain estates worked by dependent peasants for export through Baltic routes
choices.B: Oil concessions controlled by foreign companies
choices.C: Cash-crop plantations in the Caribbean
choices.D: Industrial steel production in state-owned factories

questions.correctAnswerText: Large grain estates worked by dependent peasants for export through Baltic routes
questions.explanation: Magnate wealth often rested on estates producing grain for Baltic export.

questions.prompt: Originalized Dutch report from Recife, Brazil, c. 1640: “The company’s profits depend on repairing sugar mills, securing credit for planters, and allowing merchants of several faiths to trade under protection. Enslaved laborers remain essential to the cane fields, while soldiers guard the harbor.” What was the most likely purpose of the report?

choices.A: To describe how company rule attempted to revive and protect a sugar-export economy
choices.B: To abolish plantation agriculture in Brazil
choices.C: To argue that enslaved labor was unnecessary in sugar production
choices.D: To make Recife independent from Atlantic commerce

questions.correctAnswerText: To describe how company rule attempted to revive and protect a sugar-export economy
questions.explanation: The report discusses company profits, sugar mills, credit, planters, enslaved labor, and harbor defense.

questions.prompt: Originalized Dutch report from Recife, Brazil, c. 1640: “The company’s profits depend on repairing sugar mills, securing credit for planters, and allowing merchants of several faiths to trade under protection. Enslaved laborers remain essential to the cane fields, while soldiers guard the harbor.” Which phrase best supports the claim that coercive labor remained central to the economy?

choices.A: “Repairing sugar mills”
choices.B: “Merchants of several faiths”
choices.C: “Enslaved laborers remain essential”
choices.D: “Soldiers guard the harbor”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Enslaved laborers remain essential”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows the importance of coerced labor.

questions.prompt: Originalized Dutch report from Recife, Brazil, c. 1640: “The company’s profits depend on repairing sugar mills, securing credit for planters, and allowing merchants of several faiths to trade under protection. Enslaved laborers remain essential to the cane fields, while soldiers guard the harbor.” Which broader context best explains the reference to “merchants of several faiths”?

choices.A: Atlantic port cities could include religiously diverse merchant communities tied to credit and trade.
choices.B: Brazil rejected all foreign merchants after 1600.
choices.C: Plantation colonies had no connection to finance.
choices.D: Religious diversity disappeared from Atlantic commerce.

questions.correctAnswerText: Atlantic port cities could include religiously diverse merchant communities tied to credit and trade.
questions.explanation: Merchants of several faiths and credit for planters suggest religiously diverse Atlantic commercial networks.

questions.prompt: Originalized Dutch report from Recife, Brazil, c. 1640: “The company’s profits depend on repairing sugar mills, securing credit for planters, and allowing merchants of several faiths to trade under protection. Enslaved laborers remain essential to the cane fields, while soldiers guard the harbor.” Which argument is best supported by the report?

choices.A: Chartered companies could combine military protection, credit systems, and plantation labor to pursue profits.
choices.B: Dutch Brazil was isolated from Atlantic commercial networks.
choices.C: Company rule ended the relationship between slavery and sugar.
choices.D: Religious tolerance had no possible connection to trade.

questions.correctAnswerText: Chartered companies could combine military protection, credit systems, and plantation labor to pursue profits.
questions.explanation: Soldiers, credit, company profits, sugar mills, and enslaved labor appear together in the report.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the North Pacific c. 1740–1800 shows Russian posts in Kamchatka and Alaska, Aleut sea routes, sea otter hunting areas, and shipping paths carrying pelts toward Chinese markets.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: North Pacific expansion connected fur extraction, Indigenous maritime knowledge, and Asian luxury markets.
choices.B: Russian expansion was limited to the Baltic Sea.
choices.C: Sea otter pelts had no role in Pacific commerce.
choices.D: Alaska was settled only after industrial steamships appeared.

questions.correctAnswerText: North Pacific expansion connected fur extraction, Indigenous maritime knowledge, and Asian luxury markets.
questions.explanation: Russian posts, Aleut routes, hunting areas, and Chinese markets show a North Pacific commodity chain.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the North Pacific c. 1740–1800 shows Russian posts in Kamchatka and Alaska, Aleut sea routes, sea otter hunting areas, and shipping paths carrying pelts toward Chinese markets.” Which feature best supports the claim that Indigenous knowledge shaped the trade?

choices.A: “Aleut sea routes”
choices.B: “Chinese markets”
choices.C: “Russian posts”
choices.D: “Pelts”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Aleut sea routes”
questions.explanation: Aleut sea routes indicate Indigenous maritime knowledge and participation.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the North Pacific c. 1740–1800 shows Russian posts in Kamchatka and Alaska, Aleut sea routes, sea otter hunting areas, and shipping paths carrying pelts toward Chinese markets.” The pattern shown most directly illustrates which broader process?

choices.A: The creation of new transoceanic commodity chains outside the Atlantic world
choices.B: The end of Asian demand for luxury goods
choices.C: The disappearance of Indigenous labor from maritime economies
choices.D: The isolation of Pacific trade from global markets

questions.correctAnswerText: The creation of new transoceanic commodity chains outside the Atlantic world
questions.explanation: The map shows pelts moving through North Pacific routes toward Asian markets.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Giuseppe Mazzini, c. 1830s: “A nation is not a prince’s estate but a people joined by duty, memory, and liberty. Let young Italians educate, print, and organize so that the peninsula may rise from fragments into a free fatherland.” Which audience was the speech most likely intended to influence?

choices.A: Italian nationalists and reformers seeking unity and constitutional freedom
choices.B: Portuguese naval commanders enforcing licenses in the Indian Ocean
choices.C: Plantation owners seeking credit in Recife
choices.D: Soviet planners relocating factories during World War II

questions.correctAnswerText: Italian nationalists and reformers seeking unity and constitutional freedom
questions.explanation: The speech calls on young Italians to organize for a free and unified fatherland.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Giuseppe Mazzini, c. 1830s: “A nation is not a prince’s estate but a people joined by duty, memory, and liberty. Let young Italians educate, print, and organize so that the peninsula may rise from fragments into a free fatherland.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the speaker rejected dynastic rule?

choices.A: “Educate, print, and organize”
choices.B: “The peninsula”
choices.C: “Not a prince’s estate”
choices.D: “Duty, memory, and liberty”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Not a prince’s estate”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly rejects dynastic ownership of the nation.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Giuseppe Mazzini, c. 1830s: “A nation is not a prince’s estate but a people joined by duty, memory, and liberty. Let young Italians educate, print, and organize so that the peninsula may rise from fragments into a free fatherland.” The reference to “print” most directly connects nationalism to which development?

choices.A: The spread of newspapers, pamphlets, and literacy that helped circulate political ideas
choices.B: The decline of public political communication after 1800
choices.C: The replacement of nationalism by medieval manuscript copying
choices.D: The use of radio broadcasting during the Cold War

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of newspapers, pamphlets, and literacy that helped circulate political ideas
questions.explanation: Print culture helped spread nationalist and reformist ideas in the nineteenth century.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Giuseppe Mazzini, c. 1830s: “A nation is not a prince’s estate but a people joined by duty, memory, and liberty. Let young Italians educate, print, and organize so that the peninsula may rise from fragments into a free fatherland.” Which statement best evaluates the speaker’s argument?

choices.A: The speaker defines national identity as shared civic duty and collective political action.
choices.B: The speaker argues that monarchy is the only legitimate form of government.
choices.C: The speaker rejects education as a tool of political change.
choices.D: The speaker claims that regional fragmentation is necessary for liberty.

questions.correctAnswerText: The speaker defines national identity as shared civic duty and collective political action.
questions.explanation: The speech links people, duty, memory, liberty, education, print, and organization.

questions.prompt: Originalized Ottoman constitutionalist article, c. 1876: “The empire cannot be preserved by palace order alone. A chamber with deputies from the provinces will bind Muslim, Christian, and Jewish subjects to a common law, while schools teach loyalty to the homeland.” What was the most likely purpose of the article?

choices.A: To abolish the Ottoman Empire immediately
choices.B: To defend absolutism against all reform
choices.C: To promote constitutionalism and shared imperial citizenship
choices.D: To separate all religious communities into independent states

questions.correctAnswerText: To promote constitutionalism and shared imperial citizenship
questions.explanation: The article supports deputies, common law, schools, and loyalty across religious communities.

questions.prompt: Originalized Ottoman constitutionalist article, c. 1876: “The empire cannot be preserved by palace order alone. A chamber with deputies from the provinces will bind Muslim, Christian, and Jewish subjects to a common law, while schools teach loyalty to the homeland.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the author hoped to integrate diverse communities?

choices.A: “Palace order”
choices.B: “A chamber”
choices.C: “Muslim, Christian, and Jewish subjects”
choices.D: “Schools teach”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Muslim, Christian, and Jewish subjects”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows the author addressing diverse imperial communities.

questions.prompt: Originalized Ottoman constitutionalist article, c. 1876: “The empire cannot be preserved by palace order alone. A chamber with deputies from the provinces will bind Muslim, Christian, and Jewish subjects to a common law, while schools teach loyalty to the homeland.” Which broader context best explains the article?

choices.A: Ottoman reformers sought new forms of citizenship and constitutional government in response to internal and external pressures.
choices.B: The Ottoman Empire had no religious diversity after 1750.
choices.C: Constitutional ideas disappeared from the Middle East before 1876.
choices.D: Ottoman rulers rejected all educational reform in the nineteenth century.

questions.correctAnswerText: Ottoman reformers sought new forms of citizenship and constitutional government in response to internal and external pressures.
questions.explanation: Deputies, common law, schools, and loyalty reflect Ottoman constitutional reform.

questions.prompt: Originalized Ottoman constitutionalist article, c. 1876: “The empire cannot be preserved by palace order alone. A chamber with deputies from the provinces will bind Muslim, Christian, and Jewish subjects to a common law, while schools teach loyalty to the homeland.” Which statement best evaluates the author’s argument?

choices.A: The author argues that constitutional representation could strengthen, rather than destroy, the empire.
choices.B: The author claims that only one religious community should have legal rights.
choices.C: The author rejects the use of schools for political loyalty.
choices.D: The author believes provincial deputies would weaken all common law.

questions.correctAnswerText: The author argues that constitutional representation could strengthen, rather than destroy, the empire.
questions.explanation: The article claims a chamber and common law would preserve the empire.

questions.prompt:
Table: Niger Delta palm oil commerce, simplified pattern
Period | Export pattern | Political development
1820s | Palm oil exports increase as British demand for industrial lubricants and soap grows | Local merchant houses expand
1860s | British consuls intervene in commercial disputes | Treaties pressure rulers to accept foreign arbitration
1880s | Charter and colonial claims expand | Local autonomy declines
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Industrial demand helped expand palm oil exports and foreign political intervention.
choices.B: Palm oil exports declined because industrial demand disappeared.
choices.C: British consuls avoided commercial disputes in West Africa.
choices.D: Local merchant houses had no role in nineteenth-century trade.

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial demand helped expand palm oil exports and foreign political intervention.
questions.explanation: The table links British industrial demand, palm oil exports, consular intervention, treaties, and declining autonomy.

questions.prompt:
Table: Niger Delta palm oil commerce, simplified pattern
Period | Export pattern | Political development
1820s | Palm oil exports increase as British demand for industrial lubricants and soap grows | Local merchant houses expand
1860s | British consuls intervene in commercial disputes | Treaties pressure rulers to accept foreign arbitration
1880s | Charter and colonial claims expand | Local autonomy declines
Which evidence best supports the claim that commercial relationships became political interventions?

choices.A: “Palm oil exports increase”
choices.B: “British consuls intervene in commercial disputes”
choices.C: “Industrial lubricants and soap”
choices.D: “Local merchant houses expand”

questions.correctAnswerText: “British consuls intervene in commercial disputes”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly connects commerce to political intervention.

questions.prompt:
Table: Niger Delta palm oil commerce, simplified pattern
Period | Export pattern | Political development
1820s | Palm oil exports increase as British demand for industrial lubricants and soap grows | Local merchant houses expand
1860s | British consuls intervene in commercial disputes | Treaties pressure rulers to accept foreign arbitration
1880s | Charter and colonial claims expand | Local autonomy declines
Which broader context best explains the increase in British demand for palm oil?

choices.A: Industrial production created demand for raw materials used in manufacturing and machinery.
choices.B: Palm oil was used mainly to fuel medieval caravans.
choices.C: Industrialization reduced all demand for tropical commodities.
choices.D: Palm oil was unrelated to nineteenth-century manufacturing.

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial production created demand for raw materials used in manufacturing and machinery.
questions.explanation: Palm oil was used for soap and industrial lubricants, linking it to industrial demand.

questions.prompt:
Table: Niger Delta palm oil commerce, simplified pattern
Period | Export pattern | Political development
1820s | Palm oil exports increase as British demand for industrial lubricants and soap grows | Local merchant houses expand
1860s | British consuls intervene in commercial disputes | Treaties pressure rulers to accept foreign arbitration
1880s | Charter and colonial claims expand | Local autonomy declines
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Export commerce could become a pathway from trade influence to imperial control.
choices.B: Industrial capitalism reduced European involvement in African politics.
choices.C: West African rulers gained increasing autonomy from treaties in the late nineteenth century.
choices.D: Commercial disputes had no political consequences.

questions.correctAnswerText: Export commerce could become a pathway from trade influence to imperial control.
questions.explanation: The table moves from export growth to consular intervention, treaties, charter claims, and declining autonomy.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mahdist proclamation, Sudan, c. 1881: “The foreign-backed officials tax the poor, seize grain, and mock the law of God. Those who follow the Mahdi must cleanse the land of corruption and restore justice against the armies sent from the Nile.” What was the most likely purpose of the proclamation?

choices.A: To mobilize religious and political resistance against Egyptian-British influence
choices.B: To invite British officers to rule Sudan directly
choices.C: To defend foreign-backed taxation as legitimate
choices.D: To end all Islamic political language in Sudan

questions.correctAnswerText: To mobilize religious and political resistance against Egyptian-British influence
questions.explanation: The proclamation criticizes foreign-backed officials, taxation, grain seizure, and calls for Mahdist resistance.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mahdist proclamation, Sudan, c. 1881: “The foreign-backed officials tax the poor, seize grain, and mock the law of God. Those who follow the Mahdi must cleanse the land of corruption and restore justice against the armies sent from the Nile.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religious legitimacy shaped the resistance?

choices.A: “Tax the poor”
choices.B: “Seize grain”
choices.C: “Armies sent”
choices.D: “Follow the Mahdi”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Follow the Mahdi”
questions.explanation: The phrase directly shows religious leadership and legitimacy shaping resistance.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mahdist proclamation, Sudan, c. 1881: “The foreign-backed officials tax the poor, seize grain, and mock the law of God. Those who follow the Mahdi must cleanse the land of corruption and restore justice against the armies sent from the Nile.” The proclamation most directly connects resistance to which grievance?

choices.A: The end of all local agriculture
choices.B: Taxation and grain seizure by foreign-backed authorities
choices.C: The spread of factory wages in Sudan
choices.D: The decline of all religious movements

questions.correctAnswerText: Taxation and grain seizure by foreign-backed authorities
questions.explanation: The proclamation identifies taxes and grain seizure as grievances.

questions.prompt: Originalized Mahdist proclamation, Sudan, c. 1881: “The foreign-backed officials tax the poor, seize grain, and mock the law of God. Those who follow the Mahdi must cleanse the land of corruption and restore justice against the armies sent from the Nile.” Which statement best evaluates the proclamation’s argument?

choices.A: It frames anti-imperial resistance as both a response to material exploitation and a religious duty.
choices.B: It rejects the idea that taxation can cause political unrest.
choices.C: It argues that foreign-backed officials should expand their authority.
choices.D: It treats religion as irrelevant to political mobilization.

questions.correctAnswerText: It frames anti-imperial resistance as both a response to material exploitation and a religious duty.
questions.explanation: The proclamation combines economic grievances with religious language about the Mahdi and justice.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Balkans, 1912–1913, shows Ottoman territory shrinking, Serbian, Bulgarian, Greek, and Montenegrin advances, contested Macedonian zones, and refugee routes moving toward major cities.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: The Balkan Wars contributed to territorial change, nationalist conflict, and population displacement.
choices.B: Ottoman power expanded across the Balkans after 1913.
choices.C: Nationalist movements had no role in Balkan conflict.
choices.D: Civilian populations were unaffected by the wars.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Balkan Wars contributed to territorial change, nationalist conflict, and population displacement.
questions.explanation: The map shows Ottoman losses, nationalist armies, contested Macedonia, and refugee routes.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Balkans, 1912–1913, shows Ottoman territory shrinking, Serbian, Bulgarian, Greek, and Montenegrin advances, contested Macedonian zones, and refugee routes moving toward major cities.” Which map feature best supports the claim that the wars affected civilians?

choices.A: “Ottoman territory shrinking”
choices.B: “Contested Macedonian zones”
choices.C: “Serbian, Bulgarian, Greek, and Montenegrin advances”
choices.D: “Refugee routes moving toward major cities”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Refugee routes moving toward major cities”
questions.explanation: Refugee routes show the civilian impact of the wars.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Balkans, 1912–1913, shows Ottoman territory shrinking, Serbian, Bulgarian, Greek, and Montenegrin advances, contested Macedonian zones, and refugee routes moving toward major cities.” Which broader context best explains the conflicts shown?

choices.A: The decline of imperial rule and the rise of competing nationalisms in southeastern Europe
choices.B: The spread of decolonization in sub-Saharan Africa after 1945
choices.C: The growth of Indian Ocean spice monopolies
choices.D: The formation of the Mongol Empire

questions.correctAnswerText: The decline of imperial rule and the rise of competing nationalisms in southeastern Europe
questions.explanation: The Balkan Wars reflected Ottoman decline and rival nationalist territorial claims.

questions.prompt: Originalized medical report from the Western Front, 1917: “Men arrive coughing blood after clouds drifted over the trench. Masks saved some, but panic spread when the wind shifted. The doctors write that the new weapons turn air itself into a battlefield.” What was the most likely purpose of the report?

choices.A: To describe the medical and psychological effects of chemical warfare
choices.B: To argue that World War I was fought without new technology
choices.C: To promote poison gas as harmless to soldiers
choices.D: To show that trenches eliminated the need for medical care

questions.correctAnswerText: To describe the medical and psychological effects of chemical warfare
questions.explanation: The report describes coughing blood, gas clouds, masks, panic, wind shifts, and air as battlefield.

questions.prompt: Originalized medical report from the Western Front, 1917: “Men arrive coughing blood after clouds drifted over the trench. Masks saved some, but panic spread when the wind shifted. The doctors write that the new weapons turn air itself into a battlefield.” Which statement best evaluates the report’s argument?

choices.A: It shows that modern warfare could weaponize environmental conditions such as wind and air.
choices.B: It claims that weather had no effect on chemical weapons.
choices.C: It argues that gas masks made chemical warfare irrelevant.
choices.D: It presents chemical warfare as a purely economic policy.

questions.correctAnswerText: It shows that modern warfare could weaponize environmental conditions such as wind and air.
questions.explanation: The report connects gas clouds, wind shifts, masks, panic, and the air itself as a weapon.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Mohammad Mossadegh, Iran, 1951: “The oil beneath our soil cannot remain a private kingdom outside the nation’s law. We seek friendship with all countries, but revenue from our labor must build Iranian schools, roads, and dignity.” What was the most likely purpose of the speech?

choices.A: To defend foreign company control over Iranian oil
choices.B: To justify nationalizing oil as an act of sovereignty and development
choices.C: To reject all diplomatic relations with other countries
choices.D: To argue that natural resources should not support public projects

questions.correctAnswerText: To justify nationalizing oil as an act of sovereignty and development
questions.explanation: Mossadegh links oil, national law, revenue, development, schools, roads, and dignity.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Mohammad Mossadegh, Iran, 1951: “The oil beneath our soil cannot remain a private kingdom outside the nation’s law. We seek friendship with all countries, but revenue from our labor must build Iranian schools, roads, and dignity.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the speaker linked resource control to development?

choices.A: “The oil beneath our soil”
choices.B: “Friendship with all countries”
choices.C: “Revenue from our labor must build Iranian schools, roads, and dignity”
choices.D: “Nation’s law”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Revenue from our labor must build Iranian schools, roads, and dignity”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly connects resource revenue to development.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Mohammad Mossadegh, Iran, 1951: “The oil beneath our soil cannot remain a private kingdom outside the nation’s law. We seek friendship with all countries, but revenue from our labor must build Iranian schools, roads, and dignity.” Which broader context best explains the speech?

choices.A: Postwar states used resource nationalism to challenge foreign economic power during the Cold War era.
choices.B: Middle Eastern oil had no international significance after 1945.
choices.C: Decolonization ended all disputes over natural resources.
choices.D: Iran became a formal colony of the United Nations in 1951.

questions.correctAnswerText: Postwar states used resource nationalism to challenge foreign economic power during the Cold War era.
questions.explanation: The speech fits postwar resource nationalism and challenges foreign company control.

questions.prompt:
Table: Oil-producing states and economic coordination, simplified pattern
Period | Development
1960 | Several oil-producing states form a producers’ organization
1970s | Members press for greater control over prices and production
Late 1970s | Oil revenue funds state development projects but also increases global economic tensions
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Oil-producing states attempted to increase bargaining power within the global economy.
choices.B: Oil producers avoided cooperation after 1960.
choices.C: Oil revenue had no connection to state development.
choices.D: Energy politics disappeared during the Cold War.

questions.correctAnswerText: Oil-producing states attempted to increase bargaining power within the global economy.
questions.explanation: The table shows producers organizing, controlling prices and production, and using revenue for development.

questions.prompt:
Table: Oil-producing states and economic coordination, simplified pattern
Period | Development
1960 | Several oil-producing states form a producers’ organization
1970s | Members press for greater control over prices and production
Late 1970s | Oil revenue funds state development projects but also increases global economic tensions
The pattern in the table most directly reflects which broader postcolonial goal?

choices.A: Reducing state control over natural resources
choices.B: Increasing economic sovereignty in a world economy shaped by powerful companies and industrial states
choices.C: Eliminating all international commodity markets
choices.D: Restoring direct colonial rule over oil fields

questions.correctAnswerText: Increasing economic sovereignty in a world economy shaped by powerful companies and industrial states
questions.explanation: Producer coordination sought greater control over natural resources and global pricing.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a Kenyan environmental activist, c. 1980s: “When women plant trees, they plant fuel, soil, water, and courage. The forest is not separate from democracy, for a government that sells the land also silences the people who live from it.” What was the most likely purpose of the speech?

choices.A: To connect environmental restoration with women’s organizing and democratic activism
choices.B: To argue that deforestation improves rural life
choices.C: To separate environmental issues from political participation
choices.D: To promote state control over all local associations

questions.correctAnswerText: To connect environmental restoration with women’s organizing and democratic activism
questions.explanation: The speech links tree planting, women, fuel, soil, water, courage, land, and democracy.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a Kenyan environmental activist, c. 1980s: “When women plant trees, they plant fuel, soil, water, and courage. The forest is not separate from democracy, for a government that sells the land also silences the people who live from it.” Which phrase best supports the claim that environmental activism had social and gender dimensions?

choices.A: “Women plant trees”
choices.B: “The forest”
choices.C: “Government that sells the land”
choices.D: “Democracy”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Women plant trees”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows the gendered and social dimensions of the activism.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a Kenyan environmental activist, c. 1980s: “When women plant trees, they plant fuel, soil, water, and courage. The forest is not separate from democracy, for a government that sells the land also silences the people who live from it.” Which statement best evaluates the activist’s argument?

choices.A: The activist frames environmental protection as linked to community rights and political accountability.
choices.B: The activist claims that tree planting should replace all political action.
choices.C: The activist argues that forests are unrelated to rural livelihoods.
choices.D: The activist rejects women’s participation in public life.

questions.correctAnswerText: The activist frames environmental protection as linked to community rights and political accountability.
questions.explanation: The speech connects forests, land sales, silencing people, democracy, and community livelihoods.

questions.prompt: Described social media campaign image, c. 2010s: “A collage shows protesters in several countries holding phones, QR codes linking to donation pages, and hashtags calling for labor rights, anti-corruption reform, and climate action. Corporate logos and government buildings appear in the background.” Which interpretation is best supported by the image?

choices.A: Digital platforms became tools for transnational activism and rapid political communication.
choices.B: Social movements after 2000 avoided online communication.
choices.C: Hashtags were used only by governments to prevent protest.
choices.D: Digital activism ended all local organizing.

questions.correctAnswerText: Digital platforms became tools for transnational activism and rapid political communication.
questions.explanation: Phones, QR codes, hashtags, donations, and international protesters show digital activism.

questions.prompt: Described social media campaign image, c. 2010s: “A collage shows protesters in several countries holding phones, QR codes linking to donation pages, and hashtags calling for labor rights, anti-corruption reform, and climate action. Corporate logos and government buildings appear in the background.” The image most directly reflects which broader feature of globalization?

choices.A: Activist messages, images, and fundraising tools could circulate across borders through digital networks.
choices.B: Political movements became disconnected from global communication.
choices.C: Consumer technology prevented collective action.
choices.D: Governments and corporations disappeared from public debate.

questions.correctAnswerText: Activist messages, images, and fundraising tools could circulate across borders through digital networks.
questions.explanation: The campaign image shows cross-border activism using phones, hashtags, QR codes, and online fundraising.
